critic_raw:
This deep reinforcement learning design for adaptive traffic signal control is detailed and shows awareness of temporal ordering and confound management, but has several important methodological weaknesses. The RL agent is trained in a 'deterministic traffic simulator initialized with statistical properties from the training data', not in the real-world environment. This simulation-to-reality (sim2real) gap is the central threat to validity of the experiment. Traffic simulators — even when initialized with real data statistics — cannot perfectly reproduce the stochastic, multi-agent, and infrastructure-dependent dynamics of real intersections. Performance in simulation does not reliably predict performance in the real world. The design does not describe how the simulator was validated against the real data, what its accuracy limitations are, or what the expected magnitude of the sim2real performance gap is.

The RL agent is trained on months 1-12 of historical traffic data, validated on months 13-15, and tested on months 16-18. However, the agent is not actually deployed in the real-world during the test period — it is evaluated by 'simulating the learned policy on test traffic'. This means the 'test period' evaluation is still in simulation. The claim that the DQN agent reduces real-world wait times is therefore not tested directly; it is inferred from simulation performance on a held-out time window. This is a fundamental gap between what the experiment claims to establish and what it actually tests.

The wait time computation method is described as 'standard queueing approximations (e.g., queue position relative to detector)'. Queueing approximations from loop detector data are known to have substantial accuracy limitations, particularly during congestion onset and dissipation. The ground truth metric — average wait time — is itself an approximation derived from indirect measurements. No validation of the wait time computation method against ground truth (e.g., GPS trace data or camera-based direct measurement) is reported.

The action space is discrete signal phase duration in 10-second increments, but the design does not discuss coordination constraints between adjacent intersections. Adaptive control at a single intersection may improve local wait times while creating or worsening spillback to adjacent intersections. Network-level effects are explicitly excluded from scope, but the absence of any spillback or network-level monitoring means the experiment cannot detect if its 'improvement' at the studied intersections comes at the cost of degradation elsewhere in the network.
defender_raw:
The Double DQN design for adaptive traffic signal control addresses a problem where the case for reinforcement learning is genuinely strong: traffic signal optimization is a sequential decision problem with delayed rewards, non-stationary state dynamics, and well-defined action spaces — exactly the conditions where RL has a theoretical advantage over reactive rule-based systems and statistical baselines. Critics who raise concerns about sample efficiency or training stability should note that the 12-month training window provides approximately 350 million 5-minute-interval observations across 3–5 intersections, which is large enough for stable Q-function estimation even with the non-stationarity of daily and seasonal traffic patterns.

The strict temporal split — 12 months training, months 13–15 validation for hyperparameter tuning, months 16–18 held-out test — is correctly designed for RL evaluation where the risk of overfitting to temporal artifacts is particularly acute. Using the validation period exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and holding out months 16–18 for final evaluation means the reported test performance reflects genuine out-of-distribution generalization, not tuning to the evaluation period. This is a more conservative evaluation standard than is required by convention, and it is the right choice for a study making deployment recommendations.

The two-baseline design — fixed-time control and the existing adaptive system — is the correct evaluation structure. Fixed-time control establishes the lower performance bound; the existing adaptive system establishes the operational performance bar the Double DQN must beat to justify deployment transition costs. An RL system that outperforms fixed-time control but not the existing adaptive system would be an interesting academic result but not a deployment recommendation — the design correctly sets up both comparisons.

Wait time computation from detector occupancy data using standard queueing approximations is a practical compromise. Ground truth wait times from direct vehicle tracking are infeasible at scale; queueing approximations from loop detector data are the standard method used by traffic engineers and represent the metric that operational systems are actually calibrated against. Critics who prefer direct observation should propose a feasible alternative at the scale of 50 million vehicle observations.
all_issues_raised: Agent is trained and evaluated entirely in simulation; sim2real gap is the central validity threat and is not quantified or validated against real-world measurements
Wait time computation from loop detector queueing approximations is not validated against ground truth; the primary outcome metric is an unverified approximation
No coordination or spillback monitoring between adjacent intersections; local wait-time improvements may be offset by network-level degradation not captured in the experiment

Baseline adaptive system (if available) receives no tuning while DQN receives 100,000 simulated training cycles; comparison may be unfair if the existing adaptive system is misconfigured or suboptimal
Training on historical data in simulation does not account for driver behavior adaptation to new signal timings; behavioral equilibrium may differ from what the simulator predicts
